audioscript.text: Part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman asking a friend for restaurant recommendations.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I've been meaning to ask you for some advice about restaurants.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I need to book somewhere to celebrate my sister's 30th birthday, and I like the sound of that place you went to for your mum's 50th.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The Junction?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, I'd definitely recommend that for a special occasion.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We had a great time there.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Everyone really enjoyed it.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Where is it again?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I can't remember.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's on Greyson Street, only about a 2 minute walk from the station.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that's good.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'd prefer not to have to drive anywhere, but I don't want to have to walk too far either.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, the location is perfect, but that's not necessarily why I'd recommend it.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The food's amazing. If you like fish,
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: it's probably the best restaurant in town for that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's always really fresh, and there are lots of interesting dishes to choose from, but all the food is good there.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Is it really expensive?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's certainly not cheap, but for a special occasion, I think it's fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's got a great atmosphere.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And before dinner, you can go up on the roof and have a drink.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's really nice up there, but you need to book.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's very popular, as the views are spectacular.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm, sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So that's definitely a possibility then.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Is there anywhere else you can think of?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: um If you want somewhere a bit less formal then you could try Paloma.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Where's that?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I haven't heard of it.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No, it's quite new.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's only been open a few months, but it's got a great reputation already.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's in a really beautiful old building on Bow Street.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, I think I know where you mean.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right beside the cinema.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, that's it.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I've only been there a couple of times, but I was really impressed.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The chef used to work at Don Filipe's apparently.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I was really sorry when that closed down.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So is all the food they serve Spanish then?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, you can get lots of small dishes to share, which always works really well if you're in a group.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Worth thinking about.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, there's a lively atmosphere, and the waiters are really friendly.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The only thing is that you need to pay a 50 pound deposit to book a table.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: A lot of restaurants are doing that these days.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I should have a look at the menu to check there's a good choice of vegetarian dishes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: A couple of my friends have stopped eating meat.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Not sure, I'd say the selection of those would be quite limited.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I've just thought of another idea.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Have you been to the Audley?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: No, don't think I've heard of it.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: How's it spelt?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: AUDLEY.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You must have heard of it.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: There's been a lot about it in the press.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I don't tend to pay much attention to that kind of thing.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So where is it exactly?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's in that hotel near Baxter Bridge, on the top floor.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, the views would be incredible from up there.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, I'd love to go.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I can't think of the chef's name, but she was a judge on that TV cookery show recently, and she's written a couple of cookery books.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, Angela Frayn.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's the one.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Anyway, it's had excellent reviews from all the newspapers.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That would be a memorable place for a celebration.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Definitely, obviously it's worth going there just for the view, but the food is supposed to be really special.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: She only likes cooking with local products, doesn't she?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Everything at the restaurant has to be sourced within a short distance, and absolutely nothing flown in from abroad.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I imagine it's really expensive though.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, you could go for the set lunch.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's quite reasonable for a top class restaurant, £30 a head.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: In the evening, I think it would be more like £50.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: At least that I should think, but I'm sure everyone would enjoy it.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's not the kind of place you leave feeling hungry though, is it?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: With tiny portions.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No, the reviews I've read didn't mention that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I imagine they'd be average.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, that's all great.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Thanks...
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Introduction to Pottery at Edelman Pottery

audioscript.text: Part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a potter, Heather McCallum speaking to a group of people who are visiting a pottery, a place where people make pots and other objects from clay.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, and welcome.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: My name's Heather McCallum, and I'm one of the potters who work here at Edelman Pottery.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Before we go into the workshop, I just want to say a bit about the craft of pottery.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Then we'll have a look at the equipment, and you can try making a pot of your own.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Like many people, I'm sure you know that pottery as an art form is tens of thousands of years old, and we know this because it stands the test of time.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Things like baskets and pictures don't survive in the earth in the same way that pots do.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: And even if ancient pots are found in small pieces, they still provide a lot of information about the past.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: There's no doubt that pottery has given archaeologists a fascinating insight into how ancient humans lived.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: The shape of an artifact may have been lost.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: But archaeologists can tell whether the pots were for, say, storage or cooking by examining the impressions on the clay, the scratches from tools.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: And the clay itself can reveal where the pots came from.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: When I ask people why they want to take a pottery class with me, they sometimes talk about these things, like our ancestors.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: They hope that something they create will also last longer than they do.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: That their work, whether it is good or not, might say something about humanity many years after their death.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Of course, you will all have your own reasons for coming here.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: As far as I'm concerned, what I love most is the concentration you need to make a good pot.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: That focus takes you away from the stresses of everyday life.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: If you're elderly, it's also good exercise for hands and wrists.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: And helps with arthritis, and of course, it's a fun activity for children, because it's so messy.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Here at Edelman Pottery we show you some of the basic pottery techniques, so that you can use these to create whatever you wish, a gift for a friend perhaps, like nearly everyone who comes here.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: I'm sure this is the first time you will have tried the art, so we'll keep things simple today.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Now, before we move on, can I just say a word about what you're wearing?
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: As we said in our email, please remove any watches, necklaces, et cetera.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: And put them somewhere safe.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: If you have long hair, do tie it back now.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: We'll provide aprons later, but I trust your clothes are old but comfortable.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Not your favorite T-shirt or jeans.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So now we're in the workshop.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Have a look around.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: There's a lot going on.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: To make pottery that will last, you need a Potter's wheel.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: A kiln, which is basically a very hot oven where you fire the pottery, and some tools.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: So first the kiln.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: If you look over in the far corner, you'll see one of ours.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Since their invention kilns have changed very little.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Though in the past 20 years, a lot of progress has been made in temperature control.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Basically, a kiln removes the water from clay.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: At temperatures of around 1,000 degrees Celsius.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: This allows anything you've made to set permanently in shape.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: It's a pretty ugly, heavy object that's hard to keep in a house or flat, so most people don't have one.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: You may think, 'can't I use my oven?'
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Well, that's possible, but domestic ovens don't really get hot enough, and eventually the clay will crack and fall apart.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Some people fire pottery in a fire pit outside, but bear in mind that can be dangerous.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: You also need to know about safety procedures for kilns.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: As they release toxic compounds into the air.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Every Potter needs a Potter's wheel.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: This machine is used to shape the clay into an object with circular walls or sides, such as a bowl.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Its invention revolutionized the pottery industry, allowing multiple items to be produced in a day.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Lastly, there are a number of different tools that potters use, depending on what they want to make.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: When you start, your hands can make all kinds of shapes and curves without relying on a sculpting tool.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: However, there are some basic tools that you will need to handle the clay on the wheel.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Some look very strange and have even odder names.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: That you may find hard to remember.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: Rather than go through them all now, I'll just name a few tools as we go along.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: We can provide these.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: And I wouldn't recommend spending money on them yet.
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: So, let's try making a pot of your own...
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: If you sit down...
audioscript.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part two.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 2.

utterances.text: And even if ancient pots are found in small pieces, they still provide a lot of information about the past.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: There's no doubt that pottery has given archaeologists a fascinating insight into how ancient humans lived.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: The shape of an artifact may have been lost.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: But archaeologists can tell whether the pots were for, say, storage or cooking by examining the impressions on the clay, the scratches from tools.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: And the clay itself can reveal where the pots came from.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: When I ask people why they want to take a pottery class with me, they sometimes talk about these things, like our ancestors.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: They hope that something they create will also last longer than they do.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: That their work, whether it is good or not, might say something about humanity many years after their death.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Of course, you will all have your own reasons for coming here.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: As far as I'm concerned, what I love most is the concentration you need to make a good pot.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: That focus takes you away from the stresses of everyday life.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: If you're elderly, it's also good exercise for hands and wrists.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: And helps with arthritis, and of course, it's a fun activity for children, because it's so messy.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Here at Edelman Pottery we show you some of the basic pottery techniques, so that you can use these to create whatever you wish, a gift for a friend perhaps, like nearly everyone who comes here.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: I'm sure this is the first time you will have tried the art, so we'll keep things simple today.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Now, before we move on, can I just say a word about what you're wearing?
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: As we said in our email, please remove any watches, necklaces, et cetera.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: And put them somewhere safe.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: If you have long hair, do tie it back now.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: We'll provide aprons later, but I trust your clothes are old but comfortable.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Not your favorite T-shirt or jeans.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: So now we're in the workshop.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Have a look around.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: There's a lot going on.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: To make pottery that will last, you need a Potter's wheel.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: A kiln, which is basically a very hot oven where you fire the pottery, and some tools.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: So first the kiln.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: If you look over in the far corner, you'll see one of ours.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Since their invention kilns have changed very little.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Though in the past 20 years, a lot of progress has been made in temperature control.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Basically, a kiln removes the water from clay.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: At temperatures of around 1,000 degrees Celsius.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: This allows anything you've made to set permanently in shape.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: It's a pretty ugly, heavy object that's hard to keep in a house or flat, so most people don't have one.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: You may think, 'can't I use my oven?'
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Well, that's possible, but domestic ovens don't really get hot enough, and eventually the clay will crack and fall apart.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Some people fire pottery in a fire pit outside, but bear in mind that can be dangerous.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: You also need to know about safety procedures for kilns.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: As they release toxic compounds into the air.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Every Potter needs a Potter's wheel.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: This machine is used to shape the clay into an object with circular walls or sides, such as a bowl.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Its invention revolutionized the pottery industry, allowing multiple items to be produced in a day.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Lastly, there are a number of different tools that potters use, depending on what they want to make.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: When you start, your hands can make all kinds of shapes and curves without relying on a sculpting tool.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: However, there are some basic tools that you will need to handle the clay on the wheel.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Some look very strange and have even odder names.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: That you may find hard to remember.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: Rather than go through them all now, I'll just name a few tools as we go along.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: We can provide these.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: And I wouldn't recommend spending money on them yet.
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: So, let's try making a pot of your own...
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: If you sit down...
utterances.speaker: HEATHER MCCALLUM

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part two.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Heather says pottery differs from other art forms because
options: it lasts longer in the ground.
it is practised by more people.
it can be repaired more easily.

items.title: Archaeologists sometimes identify the use of ancient pottery from
options: the clay it was made with.
the marks that are on it.

items.title: Some people join Heather's pottery class because they want to
options: create an item that looks very old.
find something that they are good at.
make something that will outlive them.

items.title: What does Heather value most about being a potter?
Most of the visitors to Edelman Pottery
options: have never made a pot before.
try to learn techniques too quickly.

items.title: Heather reminds her visitors that they should

body.title: Which TWO things does Heather explain about kilns?
options: where to put one in your home
what some people use instead of one

body.title: Which TWO points does Heather make about a potter's tools?
options: Some are available for use by participants.

audioscript.text: Part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two sociology students called Tamara and Dev discussing their research for a presentation on loneliness.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Shall we go through the notes we've made from our research into loneliness now, Dev?
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: Ok, Tamara.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: ah It's been a real eye opener.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: I had no idea that loneliness has been increasing steadily for the last 20 years.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: ah I know.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: And it's the same all over the world, the downside of a modern lifestyle, I guess.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: Did you come to any conclusions about what the reasons for the increase are?
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: Well, I assumed it was mainly an issue for the elderly, but in fact it's something which affects young people just as much.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: Mmm.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: So nothing really to do with longer life spans.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: um What about social media?
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: In my case, far from making me feel isolated, it actually does the opposite.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: It definitely does more good than harm.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: I'd say loneliness has a lot to do with the way cities are designed.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: People living in high rise flats with not much opportunity to speak to their neighbors.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: I think you're right.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: Another possible reason is that people are having fewer children, and don't live in large extended family groups.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: But in this country anyway, that all changed decades ago, and yet loneliness is a more recent problem.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: I suppose so.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: A more plausible explanation is that people are having to move around for work, and often end up living miles away from their family and friends.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: That's true.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: Looking at the studies on health risks and loneliness, there are claims that loneliness has as much impact as smoking 15 cigarettes a day.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: Or similar to the risks caused by obesity.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: But I'm not sure there's enough evidence for some of these claims.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: Well, uh what about that one in Finland, which showed that loneliness increased the risk of cancer?
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: By about 10 percent, and those findings have been supported by other studies too.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: You're right about that one.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: I was actually thinking of the studies on dementia.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: Some found no association between loneliness and dementia, and others found the opposite.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: Not exactly reliable then.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: There's been a lot of research on cardiovascular disease, and whether the loneliness contributes to that.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: Yes, I read that it was hard to reach a judgment, as the definition of loneliness varied quite a lot.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: And the responses from participants were too subjective, but there's no doubt that loneliness contributes to a weakened immune system.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: Unquestionably, the data on that is sound.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: What did you think about the evolutionary theory of loneliness?
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: Well, I thought the idea that loneliness evolved because it motivated people to be with other people quite convincing.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: Survival often depended on group cooperation.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: But I don't think there's enough evidence to claim that there must be a group of neurons in our brains which influence social behavior by making us feel bad when we're alone.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: There are a few studies which support the theory, but not conclusively enough.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: More evidence is needed.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: Mmm.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: And anyway, this theory is not really useful when it comes to solving the problem of loneliness today.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: True.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Should we look at the relationship between loneliness and mental health now?
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: Ok, so loneliness and depression are clearly related, and that's been recognized by various governments around the world.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: But unlike depression, loneliness has no recognized clinical form.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: There's no available diagnosis or effective treatments, and that's not likely to change.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: I don't think so either.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: I was thinking we should start our presentation with an example from our own experience.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: I'd like to talk about how lonely I was when I started university.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: Being away from home for the first time and all that.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: Good idea.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: Everyone will be able to relate to that, although a lot of students were probably too embarrassed to admit to it.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: Yeah, uh we could discuss ways of dealing with loneliness as well, like just talking to strangers.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: Loads of studies have shown that interactions with shop assistants and bar staff make people feel more optimistic and relaxed.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: I don't know about that, but it must make people feel more connected with their community.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: True, although you need to be a certain kind of person to be able to just strike up a conversation.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: Good point.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: We should say something about solitude, and how being alone and being lonely aren't the same thing.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: It's strange the way some people can't stand being by themselves, while others love it.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: Yeah, the research shows a certain amount of solitude is beneficial for well-being, which I appreciate.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: But being alone isn't something I actually like.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: I'd never choose to go on holiday alone for example.
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: Me neither.
audioscript.speaker: TAMARA

audioscript.text: Well let's not...
audioscript.speaker: DEV

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO things do the students both believe are responsible for the increase in loneliness?
Which TWO health risks associated with loneliness do the students agree are based on solid evidence?
Which TWO opinions do both the students express about the evolutionary theory of loneliness?
options: It should be more widely accepted.

items.title: When comparing loneliness to depression, the students
options: doubt that there will ever be a medical cure for loneliness.
claim that the link between loneliness and mental health is overstated.
express frustration that loneliness is not taken more seriously.

items.title: Why do the students decide to start their presentation with an example from their own experience?
options: to explain how difficult loneliness can be
to highlight a situation that most students will recognise
to emphasise that feeling lonely is more common for men than women

items.title: The students agree that talking to strangers is a good strategy for dealing with loneliness because
options: it creates a sense of belonging.
it makes people feel more positive.

items.title: The students find it difficult to understand why solitude is considered to be

test_text.title: The Role of Rivers in Global Cities

audioscript.text: Part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a presentation by an urban planning student about the role of rivers in cities around the world.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: It's quite hard to think of a city that doesn't have a big river running through it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you think about the major cities in the world, Shanghai, New York, Mumbai, London, they're nearly all built on rivers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When these cities were established hundreds or even thousands of years ago, the rivers were a big part of people's lives.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In 16th century London, the quickest way to get from one part of the city to another was by river.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But people also used the river for fishing, as the water then was relatively clean.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And they would also go on boat trips up and down the river just for pleasure, as a relaxing escape from the noise and bustle of the city streets.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But as industries developed and populations increased city rivers suffered.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The rising number of people meant there was a huge increase in the amount of sewage discharged into the rivers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Rivers had always been used for this purpose.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But when the number of inhabitants was so small, that wasn't such a problem.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, as cities grew to over a million inhabitants, the impact on the rivers became more serious.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In addition, other types of pollution increased as factories were built beside the river and discharged their waste materials into the water.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This got worse over time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As recently as 1957, scientists at London's Natural History Museum declared that the River Thames was dead, in biological terms.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As the water was too filthy to support any kind of life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But in recent years, as rivers lost their industrial function, cities have begun to recognize their true value, and to take steps to clean them up.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, the River Thames is now cleaner than it's been for 150 years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These days you can see seals swimming in the water, and recently people had to try to rescue a whale, which had got lost and swam up the river from the sea by mistake.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Unfortunately they didn't succeed, but the problem was disorientation rather than the quality of the water.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then, all around the world Riverside areas are now seen as prime sites for development.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Warehouses that were once used for storing goods are now being converted into expensive restaurants, and also into apartments with river views, which are in great demand and sell for astronomical prices.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In Los Angeles on the west coast of the USA, an architect has plans to revitalize the banks of the river.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And to make a park there, which can provide facilities for sports, as well as a natural environment for relaxing in.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's also hoped that the Riverside can be used for other purposes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's been proposed that facilities could be provided for displaying projects related to various kinds of art that have been produced by local people, for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the city of Paris, during the summer months of July and August, all the traffic is banned from the roads by the sides of the river, and the banks are transformed into beaches where people can relax in deck chairs under potted palm trees, sunbathe, or buy a drink or a snack while enjoying the view.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But to make the most of our rivers in our increasingly crowded cities, we need to allow them to regain their original purpose, and be used as a means of transport.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Reclaiming our streets from cars and lorries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To do this, we'll have to shift more traffic back to the river, but this time cleanly and silently.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Making the most of modern technology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Already, more than 2 billion passengers use the ferry to travel in cities around the world, like Istanbul, San Francisco, and New York.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And these numbers are set to rise further, admittedly it's not a fast way of traveling, but neither is a car when it's stuck in traffic.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Of course, passenger traffic on roads might decrease as more people start working from home.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But another recent development, the huge rise in online shopping, has meant that another form of urban traffic just keeps on growing, and that's deliveries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Trucks and vans in the city pollute and double park while dropping off parcels.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Imagine using the immense capacity of shipping to take these trucks off the road.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One freight barge can replace 44 large trucks, uses far less energy, and causes less pollution.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When the barge docks at the Riverside the parcels could be taken the last few kilometers to their final destination on cargo bikes, electric ones of course.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is already happening in the Dutch city of Amsterdam.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And in future, the final stage could even be carried out by drone, although at present this isn't allowed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Wouldn't it be great to unblock our city centers in this way?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Looking further ahead...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear part of a presentation by an urban planning student about the role of rivers in cities around the world.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: It's quite hard to think of a city that doesn't have a big river running through it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you think about the major cities in the world, Shanghai, New York, Mumbai, London, they're nearly all built on rivers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When these cities were established hundreds or even thousands of years ago, the rivers were a big part of people's lives.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In 16th century London, the quickest way to get from one part of the city to another was by river.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But people also used the river for fishing, as the water then was relatively clean.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And they would also go on boat trips up and down the river just for pleasure, as a relaxing escape from the noise and bustle of the city streets.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But as industries developed and populations increased city rivers suffered.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The rising number of people meant there was a huge increase in the amount of sewage discharged into the rivers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Rivers had always been used for this purpose.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But when the number of inhabitants was so small, that wasn't such a problem.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, as cities grew to over a million inhabitants, the impact on the rivers became more serious.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In addition, other types of pollution increased as factories were built beside the river and discharged their waste materials into the water.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This got worse over time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As recently as 1957, scientists at London's Natural History Museum declared that the River Thames was dead, in biological terms.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As the water was too filthy to support any kind of life.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But in recent years, as rivers lost their industrial function, cities have begun to recognize their true value, and to take steps to clean them up.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, the River Thames is now cleaner than it's been for 150 years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These days you can see seals swimming in the water, and recently people had to try to rescue a whale, which had got lost and swam up the river from the sea by mistake.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Unfortunately they didn't succeed, but the problem was disorientation rather than the quality of the water.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then, all around the world Riverside areas are now seen as prime sites for development.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Warehouses that were once used for storing goods are now being converted into expensive restaurants, and also into apartments with river views, which are in great demand and sell for astronomical prices.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In Los Angeles on the west coast of the USA, an architect has plans to revitalize the banks of the river.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And to make a park there, which can provide facilities for sports, as well as a natural environment for relaxing in.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's also hoped that the Riverside can be used for other purposes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's been proposed that facilities could be provided for displaying projects related to various kinds of art that have been produced by local people, for example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the city of Paris, during the summer months of July and August, all the traffic is banned from the roads by the sides of the river, and the banks are transformed into beaches where people can relax in deck chairs under potted palm trees, sunbathe, or buy a drink or a snack while enjoying the view.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But to make the most of our rivers in our increasingly crowded cities, we need to allow them to regain their original purpose, and be used as a means of transport.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Reclaiming our streets from cars and lorries.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To do this, we'll have to shift more traffic back to the river, but this time cleanly and silently.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Making the most of modern technology.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Already, more than 2 billion passengers use the ferry to travel in cities around the world, like Istanbul, San Francisco, and New York.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And these numbers are set to rise further, admittedly it's not a fast way of traveling, but neither is a car when it's stuck in traffic.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Of course, passenger traffic on roads might decrease as more people start working from home.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But another recent development, the huge rise in online shopping, has meant that another form of urban traffic just keeps on growing, and that's deliveries.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Trucks and vans in the city pollute and double park while dropping off parcels.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Imagine using the immense capacity of shipping to take these trucks off the road.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One freight barge can replace 44 large trucks, uses far less energy, and causes less pollution.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When the barge docks at the Riverside the parcels could be taken the last few kilometers to their final destination on cargo bikes, electric ones of course.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is already happening in the Dutch city of Amsterdam.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And in future, the final stage could even be carried out by drone, although at present this isn't allowed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Wouldn't it be great to unblock our city centers in this way?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Looking further ahead...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
